claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is produced by a systematic suffix-agglutination cipher mechanism: a small closed vocabulary of roots (~500-800 types) is combined with a set of suffix glyphs drawn from the word-final constraint set {y, n, l, r, o}, generating surface forms that appear unique but share common roots.
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs {y, n, l, r, o} are highly constrained and concentrated, consistent with a small morphological suffix inventory
If roots recombine with ~5 suffixes each, a vocabulary of 800 roots produces ~4,000 surface forms, approximating the ~8,261 unique types observed
Zipf exponent 0.8946 (below 1.0) is consistent with a generative vocabulary with moderate root reuse
evidence_against: True suffix agglutination in natural languages typically produces lower hapax ratios than 70.1%
If suffixes are purely cipher artifacts, stripping them should reveal a Zipfian root distribution with exponent closer to 1.0; this has not been confirmed
test: Strip word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} iteratively from each token; compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio of the resulting root corpus; compare Zipf exponent of root distribution to full-text Zipf exponent
test_metric: Hapax ratio of root corpus after suffix stripping; Zipf exponent of root frequency distribution
test_threshold: Root hapax ratio < 40% and Zipf exponent > 1.0 after stripping would strongly support systematic suffix agglutination; no significant change would refute it

tests_run.test: strip word-final glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} iteratively from each token; compute vocabulary
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If the hapax explosion is suffix-driven, removing the suffix layer should dramatically collapse vocabulary size and reveal a more naturalistic frequency distribution, directly narrowing whether the cipher operates at the morpheme or word level.
